title: Type inheritance should not be recursive
description: Recursion is a technique used to define a problem in terms of the problem itself, usually in terms of a simpler version of the problem itself. For example, the implementation of the generator for the n-th value of the Fibonacci sequence comes naturally from its mathematical definition, when recursion is used: int NthFibonacciNumber(int n) return NthFibonacciNumber(n - 1) + NthFibonacciNumber(n - 2); int NthFibonacciNumber(int n) for (var i = 0; i < n; i++) (previous, last) = (last, last + previous); The use of recursion is acceptable in methods, like the one above, where you can break out of it. int NthFibonacciNumber(int n) return 1; // Base case: stop the recursion It is also acceptable and makes sense in some type definitions: class Box : IComparable<Box> public int CompareTo(Box? other) // Compare the two Box instances..
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

logic.detection_conditions: Detect type definitions with recursive generic constraints where the type parameter is constrained to the type itself

conditions: Type declaration has generic type parameters <T>
Constraint references the declaring type itself: where T : DeclaringType<T>

never_trigger_on: Simple generic constraints on other types
logic.reasoning: Recursive type inheritance patterns (like CRTP - Curiously Recurring Template Pattern) can be confusing and make code harder to understand. While sometimes useful (e.g., fluent builders), they should be avoided when simpler patterns suffice.

should_not_trigger: // class Node<T> where T : Node<T> comment
